Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Void Order Under Ccp 473(D) & As Procured By Extrinsic Fraud Or Mistake
Off Calendar Jan-03-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance by either party.
Marina Brodskaya, French interpreter for defendant Daniel Lopez was present in court.

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling:

DEFENDANT DANIEL LOPEZ'S Motion To Set Aside Void Order Under C.C.P. 473(D) & As Procured By Extrinsic Fraud Or Mistake is DENIED. The order filed October 23, 2017 is not void and was not procured by extrinsic fraud or mistake. The order was the result of an ex parte application which was heard on or about October 11, 2017 and Mr. Lopez was represented at the hearing by Phil Kilduff. In all events, even if there were any irregularities in the October 23, 2017 order, which there were not, Mr. Lopez suffered no prejudice because he had a full and fair opportunity to respond to the order to show cause initiated by plaintiff John Cowan and appeared and argued against the order to show cause at the hearing on December 18, 2017.

In addition, the Court cancelled the French interpreter for Defendant Daniel Lopez for any and all future hearings in this case in Department 302.

Counsel for Mr. Cowan is to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling and complies with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Reyes F. Hunter, CSR#6576, email: rhunter@sftc.org.

MINI MINUTES:

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Reconsideration Of Plaintiffs Motion For Issue Sanctions Against Defendant Lopez
Off Calendar Dec-07-2016 Continued To Dec-12-16 At 02:00 P.M. In Dept 302
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel or Interested Person.

PLAINTIFFS JOHN COWAN AND TIMOTHY BRODERICK'S Motion For Reconsideration Of Plaintiffs Motion For Issue Sanctions Against Defendant Lopez - hearing will be held at 2:00 p.m. on Monday December 12, 2016. Unlike the usual law and motion or discovery hearing, this will be an evidentiary hearing. Evidence will be received on the sole issue of whether defendant Daniel Lopez, or someone on his behalf, deleted any data from his cell phone on or after the date that plaintiffs John Cowan and Timothy Broderick, or either of them, first requested that Mr. Lopez produce his cell phone for inspection. While the court does not mandate who the witnesses will be at the hearing, it is reasonable to expect that Mr. O'Shea and Mr. Lopez will testify at the hearing and, if they do not, the parties should keep in mind the admonition that is codified at Evidence Code 412.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Not reported. (302/HK)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Against Plaintiff John Cowan
Off Calendar Sep-12-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Kelly Ryan of Ryan Law Firm (626-568-8808) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party, John Cowan

Attorney, Katrina Saleen of Broderick Saleen Law Offices (650-857-9000) appears as counsel , for plaintiff/opposing party, Timothy Broderick

Attorney, Matthew E. Scherling, Esq. (914-400-3720) makes a special appearance as counsel for defendant/moving party, Daniel Lopez

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT DANIEL LOPEZ'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Against Plaintiff John Cowan is DENIED. Defendant does not maintain his initial burden of production. Defendant did not show that that the "causation" element, or publication of a defamatory statement by Defendant, cannot be established. In his declaration, Defendant does not deny that he wrote the defamatory statements online. Moreover, Plaintiff Cowan's evidence shows that the online reviews at issue were made by a user named "Daniel" on AVVO, "Daniel L." on Yelp, and "Daniel" on Ripoff Report. (Plaintiff's dec., par. 8). The substance of the reviews mirrors Defendant's complaints against Plaintiff that resulted from prior representation. (Plaintiff's dec., par. 11). Plaintiffs evidence also shows that Defendant's email address is linked to the "Daniel L." Yelp account that reviewed Plaintiff. (Plaintiff's dec., par. 12). Even if Defendant had satisfied his initial burden, Plaintiff has demonstrated that a triable issue of material fact exists as to whether Defendant published the defamatory statements. A reasonable trier of fact could conclude that Defendant published the defamatory online reviews. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Jose Rios Merida; Reporter: Sherry L. Sawyer, CSR #59761, email: ssawyer@sftc.org. (D302)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-24-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. VIVIAN LEUNG LERCHE, TEL. NO. 1-415-680-5731 SPECIALLY APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT;
NO OTHER APPEARANCES ON THE MATTER.

RULING - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT DANIEL LOPEZ' DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT is SUSTAINED with leave to amend in part, OVERRULED in part. Plaintiff's first cause of action, for defamation, implicates the conditional privilege of Civil Code section 47(a) because Defendant's allegedly defamatory statements were posted to internet websites and were intended to warn interested parties not to use Plaintiff's services. As a result, Plaintiff must plead malice, i.e. that Defendant either knew the untruth of his statements or recklessly disregarded the truth when making his statements. (See, e.g., Lesperance v. North American Aviation (1963) 217 Cal.App.2d 336, 341-42.) This variety of malice is not pled in the first amended complaint. Leave is granted to allege such malice, if Plaintiff is able. Plaintiff's second cause of action, for "willful misconduct," appears to be predicated on the violation of various Penal Code sections. It is not clear on what basis Plaintiff believes he can sue in civil court on these claims. Plaintiff is granted leave to amend to allege such a basis, if he can. Plaintiff's third cause of action, termed "negligence per se," is not a cause of action but rather a statutory presumption. (See Das v. Bank of America N.A. (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 727, 738.) Plaintiff is granted leave to amend to allege a negligence cause of action, if he is able. The fourth cause of action, for injunctive relief, includes a request for permanent injunction, which is proper in limited circumstances even in defamation cases. (See Balboa Island Village Inn, Inc. v. Lemen (2007) 40 Cal.4th 1141, 1158.) The demurrer to this cause of action is therefore overruled; one cannot demurrer to less than a full cause of action. Order signed in open court. Judge: Suzanne Bolanos; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter : Melanie Gheno, CSR # 7489 =302/eg
